title: კრიტიკული ინფორმაციული სისტემის სუბიექტის შესახებ საქართველოს კანონი
title_en: Law of Georgia on Critical Information System Security
description: The Law of Georgia on Critical Information System Security establishes the framework for identifying and protecting critical information infrastructure in Georgia. The law designates sectors and entities whose information systems must meet enhanced security requirements and establishes the Cyber Security Bureau as the authority responsible for oversight. The law aligns with the EU NIS2 Directive (Directive (EU) 2022/2555) as part of Georgia's EU approximation commitments under the Association Agreement.

provisions.title: Article 1. Purpose and Scope
provisions.content: This Law establishes the framework for identifying, protecting, and restoring critical information systems and infrastructure in Georgia. The Law applies to operators of critical information systems in the following sectors: (a) energy; (b) water supply; (c) transport; (d) banking, financial services, and financial market infrastructure; (e) health; (f) digital infrastructure; (g) public administration; (h) space. The Law is aligned with Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2 Directive) and the EU Critical Entities Resilience Directive (Directive (EU) 2022/2557) as part of Georgia's EU approximation process. The Law supplements the Law of Georgia on Information Security and establishes specific requirements for the most critical operators in the economy. Reference: Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2); Directive (EU) 2022/2557 (CER).

provisions.title: Article 2. Critical Information System Designation
provisions.content: The Government of Georgia shall designate critical information systems through a regulation adopted following consultation with sector regulators and the National Security Council. Designation criteria shall include: (a) the potential impact of disruption on national security, public health, or safety; (b) the number of users dependent on the service provided by the system; (c) the market share of the operator; (d) the geographic coverage of the system; (e) the degree of dependence of other systems on the designated system; and (f) the potential cascading effects of disruption. The list of critical information systems shall be classified and shall not be published in its entirety, though operators shall be notified of their designation. The designation shall be reviewed every two years and updated as necessary to reflect changes in the threat environment or the structure of critical sectors.

provisions.title: Article 3. Security Requirements
provisions.content: Operators of critical information systems shall implement security measures based on an all-hazards approach, addressing at minimum: (a) risk analysis and information system security policies; (b) incident handling, including detection, containment, eradication, and recovery; (c) business continuity, including backup management, disaster recovery, and crisis management; (d) supply chain security, including aspects relating to the security-related relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers; (e) security in network and information systems acquisition, development and maintenance, including vulnerability handling and disclosure; (f) policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk management measures; (g) basic cyber hygiene practices and cybersecurity training; (h) policies and procedures regarding the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption; (i) human resources security, access control policies, and asset management; (j) the use of multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions.

provisions.title: Article 4. Incident Notification Requirements
provisions.content: Operators of critical information systems shall report significant incidents to the Cyber Security Bureau and to CERT-Georgia without undue delay, and at minimum within: (a) 6 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, submitting an early warning including the nature of the incident, an assessment of its severity and impact, and indicators of compromise; (b) 72 hours of the early warning, submitting an incident notification with a more detailed assessment of the incident, an initial evaluation of the root cause, and the mitigating measures taken; (c) 1 month of the incident notification, submitting a final report including a description of the incident, the root cause, the measures taken to address the incident, and lessons learned. An incident shall be considered significant if it has caused or is capable of causing severe operational disruption of the service or financial loss, or if it has affected or is capable of affecting other entities. Operators shall also notify the recipients of their services of incidents that are likely to adversely affect the provision of services.

provisions.title: Article 5. Supervision and Enforcement
provisions.content: The Cyber Security Bureau shall supervise the compliance of operators with the requirements of this Law. The Bureau's supervisory powers shall include: (a) the power to carry out on-site inspections and remote supervision of operators; (b) the power to carry out security audits, either through the Bureau's own personnel or through independent auditors; (c) the power to request documents and information from operators; (d) the power to request access to data, documents, and information; and (e) the power to request penetration testing. The Bureau shall take a risk-based approach to supervision, prioritizing operators whose disruption would have the most severe consequences. The Bureau may impose the following corrective measures on operators that fail to comply: (a) binding instructions to remedy the deficiencies; (b) administrative penalties; (c) temporary prohibition of operation of the non-compliant service; and (d) referral to criminal prosecution where a violation constitutes a criminal offense.

provisions.title: Article 6. Administrative Penalties
provisions.content: Operators of critical information systems that fail to comply with the requirements of this Law shall be subject to administrative penalties: (a) for operators in essential sectors (energy, transport, banking, health, water, digital infrastructure): fines of up to GEL 1,000,000, or up to 2% of total worldwide annual turnover in the preceding financial year, whichever is higher; (b) for operators in important sectors: fines of up to GEL 500,000, or up to 1.4% of total worldwide annual turnover in the preceding financial year, whichever is higher. In assessing the amount of the penalty, the Cyber Security Bureau shall take into account the nature, gravity, and duration of the infringement; whether the infringement was intentional or negligent; measures taken to mitigate damage; the degree of cooperation with the Bureau; and any previous infringements. Penalties shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive. Administrative penalty decisions shall be subject to judicial review.

provisions.title: Article 7. Management Accountability
provisions.content: Management bodies of operators of critical information systems shall approve the cybersecurity risk management measures adopted by the operator, oversee their implementation, and shall be accountable for the operator's compliance with this Law. Management bodies shall: (a) have a sufficient understanding of cybersecurity risk management to enable them to effectively supervise the implementation of cybersecurity measures; (b) undergo regular cybersecurity training; and (c) ensure that similar training is offered to staff on a regular basis. Where an operator fails to comply with the requirements of this Law and such failure is attributable to decisions of the management body, the Cyber Security Bureau may require the operator to temporarily prohibit natural persons responsible for managing the entity from exercising managerial functions. Management accountability provisions are aligned with Article 20 of Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2).

provisions.title: Article 8. Voluntary Notification
provisions.content: Operators that are not designated as operators of critical information systems under this Law may voluntarily notify the Cyber Security Bureau and CERT-Georgia of significant incidents affecting their services. Voluntary notifications shall be treated with the same confidentiality as mandatory notifications. Operators submitting voluntary notifications shall not be subject to obligations or administrative sanctions that would not otherwise apply to them solely on the basis of the voluntary notification. The Cyber Security Bureau shall include information derived from voluntary notifications in its threat intelligence analysis and may use such information to inform security advisories to the wider community. The Bureau shall encourage voluntary notification through outreach and by providing feedback and technical assistance to operators that submit voluntary notifications.

provisions.title: Article 9. National Cybersecurity Plan
provisions.content: The Government of Georgia shall adopt a National Cybersecurity Plan for critical information systems, setting out: (a) the objectives and strategic priorities for the security of critical information systems; (b) the allocation of responsibilities among competent authorities; (c) measures to address the vulnerabilities identified in the most critical sectors; (d) frameworks for information sharing; (e) measures to develop capacity and to raise awareness among operators; and (f) plans for national cybersecurity exercises. The National Cybersecurity Plan shall be aligned with the National Cybersecurity Strategy and shall be developed in consultation with operators and relevant stakeholders. The Plan shall be reviewed at least every three years and updated as necessary. Georgia shall align its National Cybersecurity Plan with EU planning frameworks as part of its EU accession preparations.

provisions.title: Article 10. Peer Reviews
provisions.content: The Cyber Security Bureau shall, where possible, participate in peer review mechanisms established under EU cybersecurity frameworks, enabling the assessment of Georgia's cybersecurity policies and capabilities by cybersecurity experts from EU member states. Peer reviews shall be voluntary for the participating states and Georgia. Peer reviews shall assess: (a) the maturity of national cybersecurity capabilities; (b) the effectiveness of the legal and regulatory framework; (c) the operational capabilities of the competent authority; and (d) the level of cybersecurity awareness among operators and citizens. The results of peer reviews shall be published and used to inform improvements to Georgia's cybersecurity framework. Georgia's participation in peer reviews is part of its commitment to EU standards under the Association Agreement.

definitions.definition: An information system designated by the Government of Georgia as critical on the basis of the potential impact of its disruption on national security, public health, safety, or the economy.

An incident that has caused or is capable of causing severe operational disruption of the service or financial loss to the operator, or that has affected or is capable of affecting other entities by causing considerable material or non-material losses.
